[File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified / Midnight.take] Can an unprivileged taker, using multicall, submit two takes of the same offer O in the same transaction, where the first take passes isRatified (root not canceled), updates consumed[maker][group], and the second take also passes isRatified (root still not canceled, since cancelRoot was not called), potentially overfilling the offer if consumed tracking has a bug — or does Midnight's consumed check correctly prevent overfill across both calls? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker submit ratifierData where the abi.decode of (Signature, bytes32, uint256, bytes32[]) succeeds but with unexpected padding or extra bytes appended, causing the decoded values to differ from what the maker intended, and does Solidity's abi.decode with dynamic types correctly reject malformed input or silently accept it? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker submit a ratifierData where proof is a valid Merkle proof for offer O at leafIndex I, but the same proof bytes also constitute a valid proof for a different offer O' at a different leafIndex I' in the same tree (proof ambiguity), allowing the same ratifierData to ratify two different offers? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, after the maker has authorized a new signer S2 (replacing S1), use a signature from S1 (now revoked) to fill an offer, because isRatified checks isAuthorized at call time and S1 is no longer authorized — confirming revocation is effective — but is there a window between the revocation tx and the take tx in the same block where both could be ordered adversarially? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified / HashLib.isLeaf] Can an unprivileged taker submit a proof of length 0 (empty array) for an offer that is NOT the sole leaf (i.e., the root is a multi-offer tree), causing isLeaf to check currentHash == root where currentHash = hashOffer(offer), and if hashOffer(offer) happens to equal the root (collision or single-offer tree), the proof passes — does this create a path to bypass the Merkle proof for multi-offer trees? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker exploit the fact that the structHash in isRatified is keccak256(abi.encode(offerTreeTypeHash(proof.length), root)) — where root is the Merkle root, not the offer hash — meaning the signature commits to the root but NOT to the specific offer being taken, and a single signature authorizes any offer provably in the tree, including offers the maker may have added to the tree without realizing their full implications? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker submit ratifierData for an offer where offer.maker == address(0), causing the isRatified check require(_signer == offer.maker || isAuthorized(offer.maker, _signer)) to pass if ecrecover returns address(0) — but this is blocked by require(_signer != address(0), InvalidSignature()) — confirming the zero-address signer check is necessary and correctly placed before the maker check? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, in a scenario where the maker has signed a root R containing offer O with offer.group == G and offer.maxUnits == X, fill offer O multiple times (each time passing isRatified since root is not canceled) until consumed[maker][G] reaches X, and then attempt one more fill — does Midnight's ConsumedUnits check correctly prevent overfill, or does the ratifier's statelessness create any gap? [File: 'src/ratifiers/EcrecoverRatifier.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: isRatified] Can an unprivileged taker, in a scenario where the maker has signed root R1 (containing offer O1) and root R2 (containing offer O2), and then canceled R1, still fill O2 using R2's signature — confirming that root cancellation is per-root and does not affect other roots — and is there any state confusion where canceling R1 inadvertently affects R2?
